claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, could you please tell me how many children you would like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to claim Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Now, could you please confirm whether you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that.

Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful to know.

Just a couple more questions to confirm eligibility:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question: Is Alex in any of the following situations?

- In hospital or residential accommodation
- In local authority care
- Working 24 or more hours a week

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in hospital for a while now - a few months actually. But I still regularly buy things for them - you know, clothes, magazines, their favorite snacks and things like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital for a while.

Could you tell me approximately how long Alex has been in hospital? Specifically, has it been more than 12 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been a bit longer than that - about 15 weeks now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand this must be a difficult time for you.

Since Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, there's an important detail I need to clarify:

You mentioned you regularly spend money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). Is this hospital in the UK, and are you continuing to regularly spend money on Alex during their hospital stay?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the hospital is in the UK, and yes, I am continuing to regularly spend money on Alex while they're there - buying them the things they need and like.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all of that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm pleased to tell you that **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's a summary of my assessment:

**For Alex (age 8):** ✅ Eligible

Although Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (which would normally affect your eligibility), there is an exception that applies to your situation. Because:
1. Alex is in a UK hospital, and
2. You are regularly spending money on Alex during their hospital stay

...you continue to qualify for Child Benefit.

**Important:** You should inform HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) about Alex's hospital stay if you haven't already. You can do this by filling in an online form or contacting them by phone or post.

I hope Alex recovers well. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Although Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 15 weeks), there is an exception to the usual rule that Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital. The exception applies when the child is in a UK hospital and the parent regularly spends money on them. The claimant has confirmed that: (1) the hospital is in the UK, and (2) they are continuing to regularly spend money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). Therefore, the claimant meets the exception criteria and remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for one child, Alex (age 8), who normally lives with them but has been in hospital for approximately 15 weeks. While Child Benefit usually stops after a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, an exception applies because Alex is in a UK hospital and the claimant regularly spends money on them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant is therefore eligible for Child Benefit.

The exception applies when the child is in a UK hospital and the parent regularly spends money on them. The claimant has confirmed that: (1) the hospital is in the UK, and (2) they are continuing to regularly spend money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). Therefore, the claimant meets the exception criteria and remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for one child, Alex (age 8), who normally lives with them but has been in hospital for approximately 15 weeks. While Child Benefit usually stops after a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, an exception applies because Alex is in a UK hospital and the claimant regularly spends money on them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant is therefore eligible for Child Benefit.
